title: 2026/03/16 ENV (Part 4)
description: Monday, 16 March 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Mercury New Zealand Conservation Authority Ātiawa ki Whakarongotai Charitable Trust Environmental Law Initiative Infrastructure New Zealand Genesis New Zealand Law Society LGNZ WasteMINZ Powerco Alex Macmillan Environmental Defence Society
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. Mercury

chunks.text: everybody. Thanks for the opportunity to be here. So my name's Stu Hamilton. I'm the Chief Executive of Mercury, and I'm supported today with Christina Healey. I can see Christina there. Christina is our Principal Policy and Planning Advisor. So I'll talk for about, 10 minutes or so, and then leave plenty of time for questions. The more technical questions, Christina will answer, and I'll, I'll stick with the higher level strategic questions.

So we're, we're here to speak to our, our submission. Uh, we also have sup- support the, the detailed submission from the Electricity Sector Environment Group, which I believe, you've seen. It contains rationale and potential solutions, and guide rails we'll think will help. My framing today is pretty simple. And New Zealand needs to build and maintain renewable electricity infrastructure at scale. And we need to do that in a way that keeps operating reliably because that underpins electrification, that underpins emissions reduction and

economic growth. These bills take a funnel approach, and that means to us that the biggest gains come from getting the upstream settings right. Those are the national instruments and the regional combined plans, so that the downstream and consenting and permitting is far more predictable and less adversarial. We're conscious that dur- durable reform has to carry social license, and that's for communities, iwi, and the whole country as a whole.

And so Mercury's long-term aspiration is called Tuatara, which is to be the partner of choice, and that shapes how we develop and how we operate, and also shapes our approach to these bills. So with that in mind, there are three core points that we've made throughout our submission. The first one is to front load the effort on upstream settings to ensure the downstream decisions are reliable, high quality and predictable.

The second is to make environmental limits and biodiversity setting workable in practice, especially for complex catchments and existing operations. And the third is to keep consents and per- permits proportionate, disciplined and durable, as that will strengthen the social license that I just spoke to, spoke about.

This is important to Mercury because we're currently investing at scale, and our half year results that we presented just a few weeks ago confirmed three key things that we're currently doing. The first is a five hundred and ninety million dollar hydro enhancement program that's on the Waikato River that will provide an additional power for eight thousand homes a year and extend the life stations for many decades. The second is the Ngātamareki fifth-year thermal unit, which we are officially opening tomorrow, and it started

generating power in January. That produces enough power for about fifty thousand homes a year. Uh, and this third point is the two wind farms that we've currently got under construction. One in the far south, in Southland, and one in Northland. Both of those will produce enough power for another eighty-seven thousand homes. So those are pretty significant, three projects alongside a, a large development pipeline we have, which has about seventeen terawatt-hours of energy in it.

Just to put that into context, that would be enough to provide another two million homes if we built all that. So we're not, we're not short of opportunity. So these long-lived infrastructure investments, they sit right at the intersection of the energy trilemma, to make sure we're keeping supply secure, prices affordable, and doing it sustainably.

We have a strategic framework in Mercury, which is called Better Today, Building Tomorrow and Brighter Together, and this reform translates really well into that. We need to run the existing assets safely and responsibly. We need to build new g- new generation for New Zealand and do it in a way that communities and iwi can benefit from it in the long term.

So just coming back, I'll touch on those three core messages I spoke to. Uh, the first one was around getting the upstream settings right to unlock the speed and the quality of the downstream. And the message there is that the bill's promise of a more permissive, faster system really does depend on the upstream quality of the national instruments and the regional plans.

So we support a plan-led system where we have a clear hierarchy of strong national direction, including more standardization to improve consistency. We also support the planning bill's clearer delineation of what effects are in scope and out of sco- scope because that improves predictability. Two practical asks from our submission. First, when the national instruments are developed or reviewed, they require strong technical input.

Uh, that includes from technical practitioners, so that the standards are workable and, and implementable. Second, ensure meaningful early engagement with iwi authorities on the first suite of instruments. That's really critical to ensure that it's durable and it reduces the conflict that may or may come later. And then on spatial planning, we support identifying broad opportunities and constraints for renewable generation, but we caution against overly prescriptive zoning that could lock, site allocation and become

anti-competitive. The system needs to enable projects that are technically and commercially feasible while still managing the real environmental constraints. So our bottom line there is if we want better decisions downstream, we have to work on the upstream, providing evidence-based national instruments, implementable plan and partnership baked in right from the start. Second message, core message was around environmental limits that must lift outcomes without destabilizing existing renewable generation. And so our message there is about

environmental limits and how they land in the real world. Mercury supports environmental limits that deliver strong environmental outcomes, but limits only work if they come from cl-- but limits that only work if they come from clear responsibilities, clear compliance pathways, and a fair approach to allocation and catchments that have multiple contributors.

So in our submission, we're very direct in that place. We-- The framework needs to clearly address how limits interact with each other, especially with lawful operations currently underway, including how any allocation will actually operate. Otherwise, we'll create the uncertainty of disputes and you risk constraining generation that currently exists in ways that undermine energy security. So for Mercury, hydro is a really clear example.

If you're using the Waikato hydro scheme, if any environmental limit is exceeded at a catchment scale-The system must be clear on how responsibility is allocated and what actions are required so operators aren't subject to unpredictable operational constraints. So we strongly support adaptive management being explicitly embedded as a term, as a tool inside both the bills.

It matters most where knowledge evolves over time, and geothermal's another really good example. At Ngātamareke, that, the field I just spoke to before, field management relies on monitoring the key reservoir parameters to making sure that it's sustainable over time, and you're continually injec- in- adjusting the amount of fluid that you inject back into the field and how much production you're taking out of the field to manage the pressure and temperature of the reservoir.

And so that approach protects the resource and the environment without forcing constant consent variations. So our point here is that strong outcomes require clarity and implementability, not just ambition, and the framework should drive improvement over time without accidentally eroding the existing renewable generation. Our final message, the, the third message, is about keeping delivery settings disciplined and proportionate, and avoiding creating any new delay, rights.

So this is really about delivery and about having confidence in the system. Um, there's three key points in our submission. Firstly, appeal rights should be confirmed to matters r- raised in submissions. Allowing new matters on appeal just increases the uncertainty and cost and creates incentives for tactical behavior. Next bullet point, procedural review rights.

They need to be tight framed and short time limits, otherwise you create new delay rights for people who are not directly affected. And thirdly, permitted pathways matter. Uh, mandatory registration of all permitted activities would add an ad- administrative burden with little environmental gain in our view. It would make routine maintenance and minor upgrades really hard.

We also support time-certain pathways for specified energy activities, but only if extension and procedural review settings are tightly framed so the pathways stay credible. And a point particularly relevant to renewables is that we support retaining the planning bill's exclusion of general landscape effects, visual amenity, and views from private property. Let me bring that to life with, our Turitea wind farm and our board of inquiry process.

Our final consented layout was for 60 turbines. Uh, that was a significant reduction for what was applied for, which was over 120 turbines. Landscape and visual effects were the contributing factors to that outcome. So we accepted at the time that outcome and the project was built. It shows that we do make real changes in respect to landscape values and community concerns, but it also shows why the system needs clear boundaries and nationally consistent scope rules.

So in closing, Mercury supports the direction of these reforms. We think the bills can materially improve consistency, reduce process cost, and strengthen long-term confidence, provided the guardrails adopt, are adopted so we can get strong environmental outcomes with practical delivery. So three points to recap on, put the effort into upstream settings so downstream decisions are high quality and predictable, make environmental limits and biovesil- biodiversity settings workable in practice, especially for complex catchments and

existing operations, and keep consents and permits proportionate, disciplined, and durable because this will strengthen the social license and enables delivery. So thank you very much. Uh, Christine and myself are very happy to take your questions. Look, thank you very much, Stu. Uh, we've got our first question from Scott Willis.

Thank you, Stu. Um, sorry, I've got... Hang on, I've got a niggle here. Yeah, we can let you back there, Scott. Um, y- y- y- you- Do, do you want to, just try and sort out your audio there, Scott, and I will, just jump in with a question? Yeah, yeah.

'Cause I was quite interested, Stu, in your submission, around environmental limits and how that kind of could adversely impact the energy sector and, and, and your sector, because it's been talked about a lot through our submissions and obviously a lot of people have, varying views on it. Um, just interested how practically it currently, you know, would impact you.

You've obviously provided some solutions here. So I'm just wondering if you could just give us a, a quick, overview of that. Absolutely. Christine, do you wanna provide some technical detail there? And I can jump in. Sure, yes. Um, so we, we do believe environmental limits are necessary. We're not saying don't have environmental limits 'cause that's not feasible.

Um, what we really need to be, sort of the guardrails that need to be in place and how environmental limits are set and how they're managed is around particularly in catchments. If you're looking at a catchment scale, and, Stu mentioned the Waikato Hydro Scheme, the, the limits in, in the h- Waikato very rarely are linked to one specific person or one specific activity.

Usually it's a cumulative thing, if you're looking at environmental limits at that level. And so having the right sort of, responsibilities in place, allocation in terms of who is responsible for actioning any, changes or, or, or developments or measures that need to be put in place to improve against that limit, then those need to be spelt out at the start so that we don't find we're all inadvertently having to put constraints on the way we operate because somebody else is behaving within the catchment effectively.

Mm. Okay. No, thank you. I mean, it's quite a weighty part in your submission there, so, no, thanks for that clarification. Look, we'll try you again, Scott. We'll see if your audio's working. Thank you. Um, and thanks Stu and Christina. Um- I apologise for the muck up earlier.

Uh, look, I really get your point about, visual, visual effects and having had a wind farm declined on the only thing it could be declined on, which was vis- visual impact. I'm, I'm very sympathetic. But, look, to the, the point that you've said that national- consistent national direction is key, really I've got a couple of questions there.

Would a bipartisan national energy strategy provide that, that greater certainty that, that you think the sector needs? That's, that's one. And then a second question, spatial planning you've talked about from page nine onwards, are you comfortable that local government has all the resources and expertise available to lead on that spatial planning? And then, well, then I'll, I'll get, I'll then I've got a, one follow-up question.

Sure. I, I can touch on the, the first question was on basically strategy, right? A national energy strategy and, and maybe Christina you can touch on the spatial planning. So national, energy strategy absolutely would make a big difference, across industry, certainly for Mercury, but also more broadly because it, what it does is it sets really clear direction that we can then, get on with investing to and understanding what it looks like.

So w- we've certainly been a big advocate of that and would love to see that, come through. Now, having cross-party, support would be fantastic 'cause that, that then provides, sustainability and, and longevity of it. So yep, we'd very much like to see that, and it would make a big difference. Um, spatial planning, Christina, you could probably- Um, spatial planning I think for renewables particularly works brilliantly for existing sites. Um, where we tread into unknown territory is how the competitive nature of our s- our business, so the electricity

sector is a competitive market-based system. If we start prescribing where renewable electricity can go or needs to go, like development areas and things like that, we become a lot more constrained. Um, one, I don't think spatial planning will be able to move as quickly as the market can and as we can, and so therefore there will be missed opportunities if we can't mobilize to go where the power needs to go, where that renewable generation needs to be built.

Whether or not regional councils have that capability or councils have that capability, I think with existing renewables, absolutely, because they're already dealing with it, and I think as long as we're at the table and having that conversation with them as well, then yes, I think we can achieve good things. But I do think, there needs to be stronger guardrails around how, competition and renewables are planned for in the, in a spatial planning context.

Thank you. And have I got one sup, if I may? Yeah, one sup. Go for it, Scott. Thank you. And, I think w- what we've seen elsewhere in other jurisdictions is that, local area energy planning, which is not really spatial planning, it's, it's involving greater participation from local government but also iwi community and the energy sector, like EDBs and transmit- Transpower developers. Do you see that local area energy planning could, could improve on the spatial planning aspect to enable development and, and enable that more distributed,

renewables that we need? Potentially, yeah, potentially. I mean, I know we've been involved, particularly in the Waikato with those sort of discussions with the regional, Waikato Regional Council. So yeah, I do think potentially because you're right, those people are already at the table having the conversation about renew- renewables and electricity within their region, understanding where industry is that needs to d- decarbonize, understanding where growth is going to occur.

All of those things help us when it comes to knowing where to invest. Thank you. Thank you. Look, I might just jump in quickly with another question because obviously you spoke about the huge potential that there is within the energy sector to bring on renewable energy, and you gave some examples. Um, and I'm assuming that a lot of these have taken, you know, reasonable amount of time to get up and running at the moment. What's the, the cost do you think saving?

Have you done any kind of numbers, around that in terms of improving the resource management system to be able to get this renewable energy up and running faster? Haven't, haven't got any specific dollar numbers that I can, I can point to, but it'll be in the many millions of dollars for us. Um, I look at the, the time it takes us from a, a twinkle in an eye to actually producing electricity, so from a spark in the brain to a spark on the grid.

Um, at, at the moment that could be four or five years at best. Um, for some of our projects it could be seven or eight years. Uh, and, and at best it, it, it can probably be a couple of years. So, that sort of time in between going from eight to two years makes a significant difference. So now resource... Uh, consenting is one aspect. There are obviously a number of other things that, that are important to actually get the project off the ground, but you're talking about, you know, four, five years, in a project which

we're typically spending, you know, half a billion to a billion dollars worth that can add significant cost. And does that also, you know, cause concern around certainty, I suppose, of investment as well? Yeah, yeah, very much so. So, w-we have a suite of projects that we've got on our planning board, and we're trying to progress them all at the right pace based on how good the projects are, but consenting comes into that as well. So, potentially what it does is it means that the amount of money we're trying to investing, not ourselves, but it'll be international

investment as well, it will absolutely be affected by how fast those projects can be consented or consented at all. Because for you, for us to be putting money at the early phase- Mm... you do need to have some confidence that a portion of those projects will get through the consenting framework, or, or you do, you do get a bit gun-shy and pull back from the amount that you invest.

Or you focus on the projects that might not be the best for New Zealand's electricity grid but might be the most consentable, and that ultimately is not, not the best thing for, for New Zealand as a whole. Mm. Now, look, we're gonna have to leave it there, Stu. We've met time today. Uh, we appreciate, both of you coming on and your very weighty, thorough submission with a lot of considerations and, and lots of feedback there. So we appreciate that.

Great. Thanks for the opportunity. Bye. Thank you. Thank you. Bye. So moving on to our next submission today, we have the New Zealand Conservation Authority. And we can see you there, Edward, Wendy, and Paul. There we go. We have you all with us. Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. You have 20 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to, run us through the main points in your submission and then of course open for questions.

Tena koe Chair mana me nga member o te komiti kaiwhirowhiri. Tena ra koutou. Ko Edward Ellison toku ikoa. I am the chairperson of the New Zealand Conservation Authority. Also present online, as you have mentioned, is Wendy Nelson, deputy chairperson of the authority, and authority member Paul Nursts. We will each present separate components of our submission, starting with myself. The New Zealand Conservation Authority is here to speak to the proposed Clause 128 of the National Environmental Bill.

The authority was established under the Conservation Act as an independent statutory body with a range of functions, primarily acting as an independent conservation advisor to the Minister and the Director-General of Conservation. The authority has a role as an objective advocate on matters of national significance and interest in the conservation arena.

Clause 128 of the NEB creates a new avenue for wildlife permits to be obtained as part of a natural resource permit, and this has been ref-framed, as I quote here, "to reduce the burden on developers and other operators, we have added, have included a provision in the NEB that will enable permit authorities to authorize activities that would otherwise be unlawful under the Wildlife Act." The stated intent s- is also to streamline approvals by removing the need to seek permissions from permit authorities in the Department of Conservation for the

same activities. However, the bill does not say how wildlife appro-approvals will be determined, other than as part of a natural resource permit. So the decision-making requirements in Clause 154 to 167 of the bill that apply generally to natural resource permits would also apply to wildlife approval aspects of a permit. This means decisions will be made by the, the permit authority rather than by the Minister of Conservation or their delegate. This is a very significant change

that lessens wildlife protections, because the NEB does not contain the same protective purpose as the Wildlife Act or a similar treaty provision. Our submission traverses three aspects of the clause. One, consequences for the protection and management of wildlife.

Two, Te Tiriti. And three, underlying justification for the proposed, proposed change. I now pass on to Wendy Nelson. Ko Wendy Nelson taku ingoa. Um, as well as being a member of the authority, in my day job, I'm a biodiversity scientist and botanist, and I'm s- addressing the consequences of the proposed changes for protection and management of wildlife. The bill does not contain a wildlife-focused goal, and there's no reference to incorporation of wildlife permit considerations into the national direction, into spatial plans and national environment plans.

New Zealand has a really high proportion of threatened and at-risk indigenous species for which we have national and international responsibilities. The Wildlife Act protects all native birds, native reptiles, frogs, bats, some land and marine invertebrates, and nine marine fish. It's unlawful to catch or kill protected species unless authorized by the Director-General of Conservation via a wildlife permit.

And while resource consents may set broad conditions to manage the effects of activity on indigenous biodiversity, wildlife permits set much more specific technical requirements and often specify how are mitigation activities to be undertaken to appropriately protect wildlife. It's not clear from Clause 128 if there will be one authority issuing permits for a location or multiple agencies, and this leads to the possibility of applicants shopping around, leading to the processes for permits becoming really muddied and unclear,

both for those seeking and those administering permits.Regional councils and territorial authorities don't have wildlife protection functions, and as such, they don't have the staff with the type of expertise required to assess wildlife permits, unlike the Department of Conservation. So there's a real risk that the deficits in knowledge and expertise are not recognized by councils and decisions are not being made on the best data and information currently available.

As noted in the Ministry for the Environment briefing paper, placing more responsibility onto councils to set conditions for wildlife protection will have major flow-on effects for the councils and also for the department. Costs for these additional requirements will be passed on to these local authorities with no apparent additional resource intended to be allocated. The proposed Clause one two eight will lead to a loss of coherence in the protection responsibility for wildlife and will make protection of threatened species more complex and less

efficient. There's no recognition of the need for national oversight of threatened species currently protected by the Wildlife Act. And under the proposed, one two eight, there's no obligations for reporting or coordinating information about the decisions and monitoring the outcomes for New Zealand wildlife.

These are key roles of the Department of Conservation. Data on wildlife needs to be held in a central and coordinated system for national benefit and for consistent decision-making. Now, the Department of Conservation has developed a framework recently to enable decision-making about species, habitats, and ecosystems. It's called BioInvest, and this system is enabling transparency about decisions for priorities and expenditure, and it's enabling the balancing and weighing of risks and benefits for biodiversity of specific actions and investments.

There's a complete absence of policy framework to guide how wildlife protection and decisions will be delivered by this bill. And there's also been no consultation with the wider New Zealand community on the proposed changes affecting wildlife. So in the view of, Conservation Authority, Clause one two eight will lead to inconsistent decision-making, a lack of clarity for applicants and for those assessing permits, and loss of protection for wildlife. I'll hand back now to Edward.

Oh, Edward, you just need to pop your audio on there. Thank you for the reminder. Yeah. Uh, look, we've, we've observed through the reform process the Crown has consistently failed to engage appropriately with iwi. The rushed timeframe has cost the Crown the opportunity to engage meaningfully with iwi on these significant policy proposals. We note that the e-expert advisory group recommended that the bill should retain the Section eight of the RMA, which requires all persons exercising functions and powers under the legislation to give effect to the principles of the Treaty

of Waitangi. Including this clause in the purpose of the bill will ensure that this commitment is recognized throughout the legislation and not compartmentalized in one discrete part. We also, also note that some treaty settlements provide iwi with a role in wildlife management, including taonga species, and in some instances requires the Crown to have particular regard to the views of iwi. As currently drafted, the NEP is not clear how wildlife approvals will uphold, for example, redress mechanisms arising from Treaty settlements. I will now pass on to

Paul Norris. Uh, Kia ora. Paul Norris from, from, Relienzed, but speaking on behalf of the New Zealand Conservation Authority. I want to talk to the justification for the proposed changes. Are consents for wildlife permits actually constraining developers?

The d-data show that Department of Conservation currently gets involved with a small proportion of resource consent applications. A very small proportion of projects that need a resource consent also get a wildlife permit. In twenty twenty-two, twenty-three, thirty-one thousand eight hundred and two resource consents were issued for land use activities and subdivisions.

In this same period, four hundred and sixty-four wildlife permits commenced. Less than half of these wildlife permits were related to development, subdivision, and infrastructure activities. Over the last five years, only one business has raised concerns with ministers about duplication between resource consent and wildlife permits through official channels. In the past-- In the recent past, there has been concerns about the length of time it takes for the Department of Conservation to process permit applications. However, in the

past twenty-four months, the Department of Conservation has done a great job reducing the number of p- of outstanding concessions and permits applications, and greatly speeding up the processing of, and issuing of new permits. In the last six months of twenty twenty-four, fifty-two percent of Wildlife Act authority permits were completed within seventy days.

In the same period of twenty twenty-five, seventy-five percent were completed. It is the view of the New Zealand Conservation AuthorityTo place clear time expectations on the department to ensure efficient and timely turnarounds on applications for the wildlife permits could be achieved.

We believe a 60-day working frame would be sufficient, leaving the wildlife permit, permitting with the Department of Conservation, the organization that holds the expertise for our country. This approach, with the Department of Conservation being time-bound, would certainly lead to a better outcome for our wildlife, and also keep the skill set within the department that has exity- expertise to make these decisions around wildlife permits.

In summary, the New Zealand Conservation Authority does not support Clause 128 in the Natural Environment Bill. We consider that it would be... It would result a piecemeal decision-making. The decisions are likely to be inconsistent across the country, with decisions subject to the provisions of different natural environment plans and concerns of the different regional councils. Fragmented and inconsistent management is not appropriate for threatened and at-risk species which are categorized as such at a national scale. The Wildlife Act most

certainly needs to be re- revised, but injecting this clause to the Natural Environment Bill is not going to meet either the stated intent of reducing the burden for developers and other operators, and is certainly not going to result in protection of our wildlife. The Crown has consistently failed to engage appropriately with iwi.

The rushed timeframe has cost the Crown the opportunity to engage meaningfully with iwi on this s- significant policy proposals. The process to develop the Natural Environment Bill and Planning Bill has to date not reflected the partnership afforded within Te Tiriti, and does not meet even reasonable expectations.

I'd like to thank the panel now for taking the time to hear the New Zealand Conservation Authority's submission. Thank you. Well, thank you all, Paul, Wendy, and Edward. Look, we will go to, Georgie Dancy first. Kia ora, thank you for the submission.

Um, you talked about the, wildlife approval permits being made by the permit authority instead of the Minister of Conservation. Uh, my question is whether, in your view, the continuation of the minister making those decisions is the right way moving forward for those wildlife, permits, or if there is something else that you think would work better as an alternative.

I, I think, our submission states and the e- evidence we've given today supports the continuation of the principal authority resting with the minister, which is, of course, delegated to the, Conservation Department. It brings that national consistency framework and focuses particularly enables the, application of that, function to a- be applied in the way that the, the Wildlife Act is intended, to protect wildlife.

Thank you. Look, we'll move to Lan Pham now for a question. Tena koutou. Thank you so much for that submission. Um, I wanted to pick up on your comments around, making really clear that the Natural Built Environment, sorry, the Natural Environment Bill, does not have the same protective purpose as the Wildlife Act. And, is your interpretation that, I guess, if these Wildlife Act decisions are made under these bills, that it would be essentially more weight given to the no

net loss of biodiversity goal when it comes to decisions under the Wildlife Act? And what are the risks with that sort of no net loss concept, if any, when it comes to Wildlife Act decisions? Well, kia ora. I think you've summarized it well there. I think the NEB is about use and development of resources following on principally from the RMA, and it does have that no net loss, whereas the Wildlife Act is about protecting and protecting our endangered as well as wider wildlife values.

So that there are two distinct, ways that they function. Look, it is, it is a risk to us. It's going to be an... cause an erosion, really, in, in the, the protection of those important wildlife, values. Taonga species that we have that we need to be conscious of. Thank you.

We'll take one last question from Priyanka. Uh, thank you. Um, thanks for the stats that you've shared. I found that really interesting, particularly the fact that just one person, has sort of raised the concerns about the duplication of permits. Do you have a view on what problem this change is trying to fix?

We do. I'll let Paul answer that one. I simply, I think they're looking for a problem where there isn't a problem. Um, the, the, the issue I see, and I'm, I guess, talking a little bit as an operator as well on the Department of Conservation and other areas. We're a large tourism company.

Um, I, I believe, and we believe that, that there are good rigorous processes in place. Um, I think by making the department a little bit more time-bound in making decisions in the context of a wildlife permit, which would sit alongside a resource consent as it currently does, is probably some of thepotential frustration that, is played out, through media circles.

Um, but I think, just through those stats you'll, you'll see the department has made quite a step change in the timeliness of, going through, process approvals and/or making decision-makings around consents, wildlife permits, et cetera. So again, I personally believe a lot of the frustration that we might hear is maybe passed on, maybe based on things that happened in the f- in the past, but not in the recent times where there's been significant improvement by the department.

Thank you. Look, thank you very much for your submission today. A really thorough submission and, some valid points there, so we appreciate your time, all three of you, so thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Kia ora. So, we now welcome our next submitter, which is Te Āti Awa ki Whakarongotai Charitable Trust, and Richard Evans, I believe, is joining us.

Allow. Oh. Allow. Kia ora, just one moment. It's Helena Baker. Oh, Helena. Oh, there we go. We can see you. I'm go- Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. Uh, we'll hand it over to you. You've got 20 minutes with us, so, take your submission as read, and then we'll open up for questions, but if you talk us through the main points.

Kia ora. Kua rere atu au i te motu tere o Kapiti, ki Tararua te pae maunga. Ka tau mai ki Tararua, e rere ana mai ngā ia o Waikanae, ka whati mai ngā ngaru ki uta. Tū mai rā taku rangatira, te kākā kura o Whakarongotai. Ko Whakarongotai e here nei i ōku raukura, Te Āti Awa, Ngāti Toa Rangatira me Ngāti Raukawa, ō kupito whakarei. E mihi ana ki ngā mema o te komiti taiao e huihui mai nei ki te whakarongo ki ngā tuku kōrero. Ko wai au? Ko Helena Gerritsen Parata

tōku ingoa whānau. Ko au te tumuaki o Te Āti Awa ki Whakarongotai Charitable Trust. Ko Whakarongotai te manawa o tō mātou iwi, ko ngā tai e kōrero tonu ana ki a mātou, ngā tai tuku iho e hono ana i ngā tūpuna ki ngā uri whakatupu. For our iwi, the tides are an enduring metaphor. They remind us that change is constant, but wise navigation requires patience, balance and the ability to read the currents of the taiao. It is from this

perspective that Te Āti Awa ki Whakarongotai offers our whakaaro on the proposed planning and natural environmental bill, bills. I first want to talk about a relationship with place. I want to begin with a personal memory that reflects how our relationship with lis- this landscape is learned and carried across generations.

When we were children, we would climb the sand dunes at Waikanae beach with our mother, Ria Te Uwira Parata. When we reached the top of the sand dunes, she would instruct us to extend our arms as wide as possible, raise them towards Tutere-a-Moana, the highest peak on Kapiti Island, and then bring them back to our hearts.

She would say, "Stretch your arms to the horizon and embrace the land, the sea and Kapiti. Let the whenua and the moana hold you." That simple act taught us something very profound. The whenua and the moana hold us, and in return, we have a responsibility to protect them. Kaitiakitanga is not theoretical, it is something we inherit. Place-based knowledge and diverse iwi perspectives throughout the Kapiti Coast, iwi maintain distinct relationships with the shared

landscape, especially when we look out towards Kapiti Island. Our whanaunga of Ngāti Raukawa ki te Tonga, represented by Ngā Hapu o Ōtaki, look towards the southern end of the island and the environmental systems that shape their whenua.
